disease.name: Crohn's Disease
disease.note: Transmural granulomatous inflammation that can affect any part of the GI tract — most commonly terminal ileum and right colon. Characterized by skip lesions, transmural inflammation, fistulas, and strictures. In Kenya, Crohn's is less common than UC but increasingly recognized. A key diagnostic challenge is distinguishing it from intestinal TB, which is endemic. The presence of perianal disease is a strong clinical clue for Crohn's. Surgery is not curative but is needed for complications.
pathophysiologyDetail: NOD2/CARD15 mutations (most common in Caucasian populations, less prevalent in African cohorts) impair muramyl dipeptide sensing, reducing NF-κB activation and antimicrobial α-defensin secretion by Paneth cells in the terminal ileum

Dysregulated CD4+ T-cell differentiation toward Th1 and Th17 phenotypes drives excessive IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-17 production, perpetuating a vicious cycle of mucosal inflammation and tissue destruction

ATG16L1 and IRGM genetic variants impair autophagy, compromising the clearance of intracellular pathogens, particularly adherent-invasive Escherichia coli (AIEC), which is overrepresented in Crohn's mucosa
Paneth cell dysfunction reduces secretion of α-defensins (HD5, HD6), altering the antimicrobial milieu and promoting dysbiosis in the crypt microenvironment of the terminal ileum
Dysbiosis features reduced Firmicutes diversity (especially Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, a butyrate-producing anti-inflammatory species) and expansion of Proteobacteria and Enterobacteriaceae
Loss of oral tolerance to commensal microbiota triggers an inappropriate adaptive immune response targeting the intestinal epithelium, driven by defective regulatory T-cell (Treg) function

Transmural inflammation extends through mucosa, submucosa, muscularis propria, and serosa — this full-thickness involvement distinguishes Crohn's from UC and underlies the propensity for fistula and abscess formation

Non-caseating granulomas form when epithelioid macrophages aggregate around persistent antigenic stimuli but fail to eliminate the inciting agent; found in only 30-60% of cases but pathognomonic when present
Skip lesions with segments of normal intervening mucosa reflect the patchy, segmental nature of inflammation — the rectum is often spared in Crohn's, unlike UC where rectal involvement is universal

Creeping fat (fat wrapping) — hypertrophied mesenteric adipose tissue that progressively envelops >50% of the bowel circumference — is a pathognomonic macroscopic feature strongly associated with transmural inflammation and fibrosis

Progressive fibrosis results from chronic activation of subepithelial myofibroblasts and mesenchymal cells by TGF-β and IL-13, depositing excessive collagen and causing stricture formation in 30-50% of patients within 10 years

Fistulogenesis begins with transmural inflammation eroding through the serosa, forming sinus tracts that penetrate adjacent structures — the mechanism involves upregulated MMP expression and downregulated TIMP activity

Vascular adhesion molecules (MAdCAM-1, VCAM-1) are upregulated on gut endothelial cells, promoting α4β7 integrin-mediated lymphocyte homing to inflamed intestinal segments — the therapeutic target of Vedolizumab
Neural hyperplasia and ganglionitis in the myenteric and submucosal plexuses contribute to dysmotility, visceral hypersensitivity, and abdominal pain characteristic of active disease

Matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-1, MMP-3, MMP-9) degrade extracellular matrix components, while tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases (TIMPs) are dysregulated, shifting the equilibrium toward proteolytic tissue destruction

Cigarette smoke extract directly impairs autophagy, reduces Paneth cell antimicrobial function, and alters the gut microbiome composition — providing a mechanistic basis for why smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor for disease progression

Thrombocytosis and platelet activation are driven by IL-6 stimulation of megakaryopoiesis and contribute to the hypercoagulable state; platelet counts correlate with disease activity and normalize with successful treatment

landmarkTrials.intervention: Infliximab 5 mg/kg maintenance vs placebo (responders to induction)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Maintenance group: 39% in remission at week 30 vs 17% placebo (p<0.001). Fewer hospitalizations and surgeries in maintenance arm.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established anti-TNF maintenance therapy as standard of care for moderate-severe Crohn's disease. Transformed treatment paradigm from episodic to scheduled maintenance.

landmarkTrials.intervention: Infliximab + Azathioprine vs Infliximab alone vs Azathioprine alone
landmarkTrials.outcome: Combination therapy: 56.8% steroid-free remission at week 26 vs 44.4% infliximab alone vs 30% azathioprine alone (p<0.001). Mucosal healing also superior in combination arm.
landmarkTrials.impact: Landmark trial establishing superiority of combination therapy over monotherapy. Current guidelines recommend combination anti-TNF + immunomodulator as first-line biologic strategy.

landmarkTrials.intervention: Adalimumab 40 mg weekly vs every other week vs placebo (maintenance after induction)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Remission at week 56: 36% weekly, 41% every other week vs 12% placebo (p<0.001). Fistula healing also superior with adalimumab.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established adalimumab as effective maintenance therapy for Crohn's disease. Provided an alternative anti-TNF for patients who lost response or were intolerant to infliximab.

landmarkTrials.population: 526 patients with moderate-to-severe Crohn's refractory to anti-TNF therapy
landmarkTrials.intervention: Ustekinumab (IL-12/23 p40 inhibitor) intravenous induction followed by subcutaneous maintenance
landmarkTrials.outcome: Week 6 clinical response: 39.7% ustekinumab vs 23.5% placebo (p<0.001). Maintenance through week 22 also significant.
landmarkTrials.impact: First biologic with novel mechanism (anti-IL-12/23) proven effective in anti-TNF failures. Pioneered the concept of targeting the IL-23/Th17 pathway in Crohn's disease.

landmarkTrials.outcome: Week 6 clinical remission: 14.5% vedolizumab vs 6.8% placebo (p=0.02). Week 52 remission: 39% vedolizumab (8-weekly) vs 21.6% placebo (p<0.001).
landmarkTrials.impact: First gut-selective biologic (no systemic immunosuppression) approved for Crohn's. Important safety advantage — lower infection risk compared to anti-TNF agents. Particularly valuable in the Kenyan context where TB is endemic.

landmarkTrials.population: 174 patients after ileocolic resection for Crohn's disease
landmarkTrials.intervention: Colonoscopy at 6 months with treatment escalation for recurrence (Rutgeerts score ≥2) vs standard care
landmarkTrials.outcome: Endoscopic recurrence at 18 months: 49% in active surveillance vs 67% in standard care (p=0.03). Early treatment of recurrence reduced progression.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established role of early postoperative endoscopic surveillance and treatment escalation. Current standard: colonoscopy at 6-12 months post-resection with therapy adjustment based on Rutgeerts score.

landmarkTrials.intervention: Adalimumab vs placebo for fistula healing (maintenance phase after open-label induction)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Complete fistula closure at week 56: 33% adalimumab vs 13% placebo (p=0.043). Sustained closure over 2 years in responders.
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed that anti-TNF therapy is effective for fistula healing and closure. Combined with seton drainage, remains the standard for fistulizing perianal Crohn's disease.

landmarkTrials.population: 185 patients with Crohn's disease stable on infliximab
landmarkTrials.intervention: Switching from originator infliximab to CT-P13 (biosimilar) vs continued originator
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in efficacy, safety, or immunogenicity at 1 year. Drug survival and remission rates comparable between groups.
landmarkTrials.impact: Demonstrated safety and efficacy of biosimilar transition. Critical for cost-effective biologic therapy in resource-limited settings like Kenya, where biosimilars (Remsima, Inflectra) enable broader access to anti-TNF treatment.

evidenceTable.indication: First-line investigation for suspected Crohn's; allows direct visualization, biopsy, and assessment of disease extent
Best non-invasive modality for small bowel disease, fistulas, abscesses, and stricture characterization (active vs fibrotic)
Alternative to MRE; superior for abscess detection; preferred in acute setting where rapid assessment is needed
Gold standard for perianal fistula assessment — Parks classification guides surgical planning
Non-invasive biomarker distinguishing IBD from IBS; correlates with endoscopic activity; useful for monitoring treatment response
Detect proximal small bowel disease when ileocolonoscopy and MRE are negative; risk of capsule retention if stricture present
Serologic marker supporting Crohn's diagnosis; more specific when combined with negative pANCA in differentiating from UC
Mandatory screening before anti-TNF therapy in endemic TB settings like Kenya; positive test requires latent TB treatment before biologic initiation
Useful first-line in Kenya for bowel wall thickness, mesenteric lymphadenopathy, abscess detection; operator-dependent but inexpensive and radiation-free

evidenceTable.test: Upper GI Series with Small Bowel Follow-Through
evidenceTable.indication: Historical gold standard now replaced by MRE/CTE; still useful in resource-limited Kenyan settings where advanced imaging unavailable
Non-caseating granulomas are pathognomonic but found in only 30-60%; multiple biopsies from each segment increase yield

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical Suspicion — Chronic diarrhea (>4 weeks), abdominal pain (especially RLQ), weight loss, perianal disease. Consider Crohn's when symptoms persist despite negative stool cultures and TB workup.

Step 2: Exclude Infection — Stool microscopy and culture (×3), Quantiferon Gold TB test, C. difficile toxin. In Kenya, intestinal TB is the most critical mimic — empiric anti-TB trial is NOT recommended; obtain histological confirmation.
Step 3: Inflammatory Markers — CRP, Fecal Calprotectin. Elevated calprotectin (>250 mcg/g) with compatible symptoms strongly suggests IBD. Normal calprotectin makes active Crohn's unlikely.

Step 4: Ileocolonoscopy with Ileal Intubation — Gold standard for diagnosis. Inspect entire colon and terminal ileum. Document extent, severity, skip lesions. Take 2-4 biopsies every 10 cm from each segment including normal-appearing mucosa.

Step 5: Small Bowel Imaging — MRE (preferred) or CT Enterography. Assess for: mural thickening, hyperenhancement, comb sign (active inflammation); strictures with pre-stenotic dilation; fistulas and abscesses. Differentiate active vs fibrotic strictures.

Step 6: Perianal Assessment — If perianal disease suspected: Pelvic MRI (gold standard) or endoanal ultrasound. Parks classification of fistula tracts guides surgical management. EUA (Examination Under Anaesthesia) for abscess drainage and seton placement.
Step 7: Serology — ASCA/pANCA panel. ASCA+/pANCA− supports Crohn's; ASCA−/pANCA+ supports UC. Limited diagnostic accuracy but helpful in indeterminate colitis. Not essential for diagnosis.

Step 8: Capsule Endoscopy — If high suspicion persists despite negative ileocolonoscopy and MRE. Contraindicated if known or suspected stricture (risk of capsule retention — perform patency capsule first).

Step 9: Histopathology Review — Pathognomonic features: non-caseating granulomas, transmural inflammation, fissuring ulcers, skip lesions. Granulomas are diagnostic but their absence does NOT exclude Crohn's. TB-PCR on biopsy tissue if intestinal TB suspected.

Step 10: Classify Disease — Montreal classification: Age at diagnosis (A1<16, A2 17-40, A3>40), Location (L1 terminal ileal, L2 colonic, L3 ileocolonic, L4 isolated upper disease), Behavior (B1 non-stricturing/non-penetrating, B2 stricturing, B3 penetrating, p perianal modifier).

managementPearls.pearl: Always exclude intestinal TB before starting steroids or anti-TNF in the Kenyan context. Use colonoscopic biopsy for TB-PCR and histology (acid-fast bacilli, caseating granulomas). A positive Quantiferon Gold alone is not sufficient to diagnose intestinal TB — it only indicates latent infection. Tissue diagnosis is essential.
managementPearls.evidence: WHO guidelines for TB in IBD; KASL consensus

managementPearls.pearl: Anti-TNF therapy (Infliximab 5mg/kg) is most effective when started early in the disease course — the 'top-down' approach. Delaying biologic therapy until multiple steroid courses have failed leads to worse long-term outcomes, higher complication rates, and more surgeries.
managementPearls.evidence: D'Haens et al., Lancet 2008; TOP-DOWN trial

managementPearls.pearl: Combination therapy (Infliximab + Azathioprine) is superior to either agent alone (SONIC trial). Start Azathioprine 2-2.5mg/kg/day and Infliximab simultaneously. Check TPMT genotype or phenotype before starting azathioprine to predict toxicity risk.

In fibrotic strictures, anti-TNF therapy is ineffective and may delay necessary surgery. Differentiate active-inflammatory from fibrotic strictures using MRE: active shows hyperenhancement and comb sign; fibrotic shows narrowed segment without enhancement. Balloon dilation is an option for short (<5cm) accessible strictures.

Postoperative recurrence is nearly universal without prophylaxis — 90% endoscopic recurrence at 1 year. Start Azathioprine or anti-TNF within 2-4 weeks of surgery. Smoking cessation is non-negotiable. Colonoscopy at 6-12 months post-op for Rutgeerts scoring guides treatment escalation.

For perianal fistulizing disease: combined surgical (seton drainage) and medical (anti-TNF) approach is superior to either alone. Setons control sepsis; Infliximab promotes fistula healing. Do NOT perform fistulotomy for complex Crohn's fistulas — risk of incontinence and non-healing.

Exclusive Enteral Nutrition (EEN) is effective for induction of remission in pediatric Crohn's (60-80% response) but less accepted in adults. In Kenya, where cost of formula may be prohibitive, consider partial enteral nutrition with a low-residue diet as a more feasible alternative.
managementPearls.evidence: Ruemmele et al., JPGN 2014; ESPGHAN guidelines

managementPearls.pearl: Vedolizumab (gut-selective anti-α4β7 integrin) is particularly valuable in Kenya and other TB-endemic regions because it does not cause systemic immunosuppression. TB reactivation risk is significantly lower than with anti-TNF agents. Consider as first-line biologic in patients with TB risk factors.
managementPearls.context: Biologic selection in endemic TB areas
managementPearls.evidence: GEMINI 2 trial; Ng et al., Nat Rev Gastroenterol Hepatol 2016

managementPearls.pearl: In patients presenting with acute abdomen and suspected Crohn's, always consider free perforation (requires emergency laparotomy) vs contained perforation with abscess formation (percutaneous drainage + antibiotics + delayed elective surgery). CT is essential for differentiation.
managementPearls.evidence: Berg et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2002

managementPearls.pearl: Laparoscopic ileocolic resection is preferred over open surgery for terminal ileal Crohn's when feasible. Benefits: shorter hospital stay (3-4 days vs 6-8 days), less postoperative ileus, fewer wound complications, and earlier return to normal activity. Conversion rates are higher in Crohn's due to inflammatory masses and fistulas.

Corticosteroid dependency — defined as inability to taper steroids below 10mg/day prednisolone within 3 months without relapse — is an indication to escalate therapy to biologics. Prolonged steroid use causes significant morbidity: osteoporosis, diabetes, hypertension, cataracts, and increased infection risk.

operativeNuances.nuance: When performing ileocolic resection for Crohn's, the mesentery is often thickened, inflamed, and friable — use a vessel-sealing device (LigaSure/Harmonic) rather than suture ligation to reduce bleeding and operative time. The 'creeping fat' should be included in the resected specimen as it correlates with active inflammation.
operativeNuances.evidence: Strong et al., J Gastrointest Surg 2015

operativeNuances.nuance: For strictureplasty: the bowel wall in Crohn's is thickened but pliable. Use Heineke-Mikulicz for strictures <10cm and Finney for 10-25cm. Michelassi (side-to-side isoperistaltic) for long segment or multiple adjacent strictures. The key principle is to preserve bowel length — avoid excessive resection.

In laparoscopic ileocolic resection for Crohn's, enterotomy and specimen extraction through a Pfannenstiel incision reduces wound complications and provides excellent cosmesis. Protect the extraction site with a wound retractor to minimize surgical site infection risk.

For perianal fistulizing Crohn's, the single most important surgical principle is: place a non-cutting seton for all complex fistulas traversing the sphincter complex. Cutting setons should be avoided — they cause sphincter damage and incontinence. Setons remain in place until fistula healing is confirmed (often 6-12 months of anti-TNF therapy).
operativeNuances.evidence: Parks classification; Williams et al., Br J Surg 2018

operativeNuances.nuance: When operating on a Crohn's patient, always perform a complete small bowel run to identify all strictures and areas of active disease from the ligament of Treitz to the ileocecal valve. Mark strictures with sutures for identification during the same procedure. Up to 30% of patients have synchronous strictures not apparent on preoperative imaging.

For enteroenteric fistulas (e.g., ileosigmoid fistula): do NOT primarily resect the non-diseased target organ. Separate the fistulous connection, repair the non-Crohn's side primarily, and resect the diseased Crohn's segment. This avoids unnecessary colonic resection and preserves bowel length.
operativeNuances.evidence: Brooke et al., Ann R Coll Surg Engl 2010

operativeNuances.nuance: In emergency laparotomy for Crohn's perforation: the perforation site is often in the terminal ileum. Limited resection with primary anastomosis is appropriate if the patient is stable, well-nourished, and not on high-dose steroids. Otherwise, create an end ileostomy with mucous fistula — second-stage reconstruction at 3-6 months when inflammation and nutrition have improved.
operativeNuances.evidence: Berg et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2002

operativeNuances.nuance: Bowel anastomosis in Crohn's: use a wide side-to-side (functional end-to-end) stapled anastomosis rather than end-to-end handsewn. The wider lumen reduces the risk of stricture at the anastomosis. Stapled technique results in lower anastomotic leak rates in Crohn's patients compared to handsewn (4.8% vs 8.1% in meta-analyses).

prognosis.naturalHistory: Crohn's disease follows a relapsing-remitting course with progressive bowel damage over time. Within 10 years of diagnosis: 50% develop stricturing or penetrating complications, 50-70% require at least one surgical resection, and 30-50% develop postoperative recurrence. Disease behavior changes over time — more patients progress from B1 (inflammatory) to B2 (stricturing) or B3 (penetrating) phenotype with longer disease duration.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: Postoperative endoscopic recurrence (Rutgeerts score ≥2) at 1 year: 50-70% without prophylaxis, 20-35% with anti-TNF prophylaxis. Clinical recurrence at 5 years: 30-50%. Reoperation rate at 10 years: 30-40%. Smoking increases recurrence risk by 2-3 fold.
prognosis.cancerRisk: Colorectal cancer risk in colonic Crohn's is similar to UC — 2-3 fold increased risk after 8-10 years of disease. Small bowel adenocarcinoma risk is 10-100 fold increased but absolute risk is low (0.3% at 25 years). Surveillance colonoscopy every 1-2 years after 8 years of colonic involvement is recommended. Crohn's-related fistulas may rarely (<1%) undergo malignant transformation — biopsy any suspicious fistula tract.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: Crohn's significantly impacts quality of life, with 40-50% of patients reporting impaired work productivity. Fatigue is the most commonly reported symptom affecting daily function. In Kenya, cultural stigma around chronic illness and stomas can lead to social isolation. Support groups and patient education are essential for psychosocial well-being.
prognosis.mortality: Overall mortality is slightly increased compared to the general population (standardized mortality ratio 1.2-1.5). Excess mortality is primarily from: severe infections (especially in immunosuppressed patients), colorectal cancer (if no surveillance), postoperative complications, and pulmonary embolism. Early and effective disease-modifying therapy reduces mortality risk.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with worse prognosis: smoking (most important), young age at onset (<16 years), extensive disease, penetrating phenotype, perianal involvement, deep ulcers on endoscopy, severe malnutrition (albumin <30 g/L), and multiple prior surgeries. Protective factors: non-smoking, early effective therapy (top-down approach), mucosal healing achievement, maintenance therapy adherence.

presentingComplaints: Chronic diarrhea lasting more than 4 weeks, often non-bloody
Persistent abdominal pain in the right lower quadrant or periumbilical region
Unintentional weight loss despite normal appetite

history_questions.question: How long have you had diarrhea — days, weeks, months, or years?
Is the diarrhea watery, bloody, or mixed? How many episodes per day?
Where is the abdominal pain located — right lower side or around the belly button?
Does the pain get worse after eating?
Have you lost weight unintentionally in the past 6 months?
Do you have any anal problems like tags, fissures, or recurring abscesses?
Have you ever had a fistula — an abnormal tunnel from the bowel to the skin or bladder?
Do you have any family members with Crohn's disease or inflammatory bowel disease?
Do you smoke? If so, how many cigarettes per day?
Have you ever been treated for tuberculosis? Did the treatment finish?
Do you have joint pain, skin rashes, or eye inflammation?
Have you had previous abdominal surgeries? For what reason?
Have you used anti-inflammatory medications (Ibuprofen, Diclofenac) frequently?
Have you taken any antibiotics recently that might have triggered this?
Do you have mouth ulcers or sores?
Have you ever had a colonoscopy with biopsy done?
What medications have you tried for this condition (steroids, mesalamine, traditional herbs)?
Do you have nausea or vomiting after meals suggesting a stricture?
Have you noticed any drainage from around your anus or from a previous surgical wound?
Do you feel like your bowel doesn't empty completely?
Have you had any blood transfusions before?
Do you use traditional herbal remedies (like mchunga, mwarobaine, or others) for your abdominal complaints? How long have you used them?
What does your typical daily diet consist of — ugali, rice, chapati, githeri? Have you changed your eating habits recently?
Do you have access to a clean latrine or toilet at home? Have you had any issues with water quality or sanitation?
Have you ever been tested for HIV? Do you know your status?
In women: have you noticed any changes in your menstrual cycle? Do you have pain during intercourse (dyspareunia), which could suggest pelvic involvement?

examination_findings.finding: Right lower quadrant mass or tenderness — thickened ileocecal loop
Perianal skin tags, fissures, or fistulous openings
Abdominal guarding or distension in obstructive episodes
Signs of malnutrition — temporal wasting, thin habitus
Digital rectal exam — may reveal fistula tract, stricture, or mass
Erythema nodosum — tender red nodules on shins
Signs of anemia — pallor, glossitis, angular stomatitis
Absent bowel sounds in obstructed phase
Abdominal wall fistula with visible enteric drainage — classic for penetrating Crohn's disease
Oral thrush or aphthous stomatitis — oral ulcers on buccal mucosa or tongue
Perianal induration, sacral edema, or fluctuant mass suggesting deep pelvic sepsis
Temporal wasting, muscle atrophy, and generalized signs of chronic illness and malnutrition

bedside_scores.name: Harvey-Bradshaw Index (HBI)

components.name: Number of liquid stools per day
Complications (arthralgia, uveitis, erythema nodosum, apthous ulcers, pyoderma, anal fissure, new fistula, abscess — 1 each)

bedside_scores.interpretation: <5: remission; 5-7: mild; 8-16: moderate; >16: severe. Used for disease activity monitoring.

riskFactors.note: Strongest modifiable risk factor — worsens disease course

subtypes.name: Ileocolic Crohn's Disease
subtypes.management: Anti-TNF (Infliximab, Adalimumab) as first-line biologic. Immunomodulators (Azathioprine 2-2.5mg/kg). Ileocolic resection for stricture/obstruction — laparoscopic approach. Post-operative prophylaxis (Azathioprine or Anti-TNF) to prevent recurrence (high risk within 1 year).

subtypes.name: Colonic Crohn's Disease
subtypes.management: Anti-TNF therapy (Infliximab). Immunomodulators (Azathioprine, Methotrexate). Corticosteroids for flares (Budesonide MMX for left-sided). Colectomy with ileorectal anastomosis or end ileostomy for severe/refractory disease. Endoscopic surveillance for colorectal cancer after 8-10 years.

subtypes.name: Perianal Crohn's Disease
subtypes.management: Seton drainage of all fistulas (non-cutting setons). Anti-TNF (Infliximab 5mg/kg) — first line for fistulizing disease. Examination under anaesthesia (EUA) for abscess drainage. Proctectomy as last resort for refractory disease. Stem cell therapy (experimental) for complex fistulas.

subtypes.name: Esophagogastroduodenal Crohn's Disease
subtypes.management: Upper GI endoscopy with biopsies for diagnosis. Proton pump inhibitors for symptomatic relief. Systemic corticosteroids for active inflammation. Anti-TNF therapy (Infliximab) for moderate-severe disease. Endoscopic dilation for strictures. Surgery (gastrojejunostomy or strictureplasty) reserved for medically refractory obstruction.

subtypes.name: Jejunoileitis (Proximal Small Bowel Crohn's)
subtypes.management: Anti-TNF (Infliximab/Adalimumab) as first-line biologic. Exclusive enteral nutrition for nutritional optimization. Corticosteroids for flares. Multiple strictureplasties for fibrotic strictures — bowel-preserving strategy critical. Vitamin B12, iron, calcium, and vitamin D supplementation. TPN for severe malnutrition. Short bowel syndrome prevention by minimizing resections.

bedside.name: Perianal examination
bedside.indication: assess for skin tags, fissures, fistulous openings, abscesses

bedside.name: Digital rectal examination
bedside.indication: assess sphincter tone, rectal strictures, fistula tracts

bedside.name: Stool culture to rule out infection
bedside.indication: exclude bacterial, parasitic, and C. difficile infection

laboratory.name: CRP
laboratory.indication: inflammatory marker — correlates with disease activity

laboratory.name: Complete Blood Count
laboratory.indication: anemia, thrombocytosis (platelet count correlates with activity)

laboratory.name: Albumin
laboratory.indication: nutritional status — low albumin is poor prognostic sign

laboratory.name: Fecal Calprotectin
laboratory.indication: intestinal inflammation marker — correlates with endoscopic activity

laboratory.name: ASCA / pANCA
laboratory.indication: serologic markers — ASCA+ in Crohn's, pANCA+ in UC

laboratory.name: Vitamin B12, Folate, Iron studies
laboratory.indication: assess for deficiency from terminal ileal disease

imaging.name: Colonoscopy with ileal intubation + multiple biopsies
imaging.indication: gold standard — assess extent, severity, skip lesions, granulomas

imaging.name: MR Enterography (MRE)
imaging.indication: assess small bowel disease, fistulas, abscesses, strictures

imaging.name: CT Enterography
imaging.indication: alternative to MRE; better for abscess detection

imaging.name: Pelvic MRI
imaging.indication: gold standard for perianal fistula assessment (Parks classification)

imaging.name: Capsule Endoscopy
imaging.indication: if suspected small bowel disease but negative colonoscopy and MRE

imaging.name: Upper GI series with small bowel follow-through
imaging.indication: assess for jejunal and ileal strictures (historical)

interpretation_rules.fecal_calprotectin: >250 mcg/g suggests active mucosal inflammation. Useful for monitoring treatment response and predicting relapse. Not specific for Crohn's — elevated in any colitis.
interpretation_rules.mre_active_vs_fibrotic_stricture: Active inflammation shows mural hyperenhancement, wall thickening, and mesenteric fat stranding (comb sign). Fibrotic stricture shows narrowed segment without enhancement — determines medical vs surgical management.
interpretation_rules.perianal_mri_parks_classification: Describes fistula tract in relation to sphincters: intersphincteric (most common), transsphincteric, suprasphincteric, extrasphincteric. Guides surgical approach.
interpretation_rules.granuloma_significance: Non-caseating granulomas are pathognomonic but found in only 30-60%. Their absence does NOT exclude Crohn's.
interpretation_rules.anti_tnf_screening: Before starting Infliximab/Adalimumab: Quantiferon TB test, Hepatitis B/C serology, Chest X-ray. Treat latent TB before starting therapy.

management.initial: Assess disease location and activity (endoscopy, MRE, biomarkers). Exclude TB and other infections. Nutritional optimization (dietician referral). Smoking cessation counseling. Start medical therapy based on severity.
management.definitive: Mild-moderate: Corticosteroids (Budesonide 9mg for ileocecal, Prednisolone for colonic). Oral 5-ASA (limited evidence). Moderate-severe: Anti-TNF (Infliximab 5mg/kg induction at 0,2,6 weeks then 8-weekly). Immunomodulator (Azathioprine 2-2.5mg/kg/day). Ustekinumab or Vedolizumab as alternative biologics. Severe/fulminant: IV steroids, IV Infliximab. Emergent surgery for complications. Surgical: Strictureplasty for fibrotic strictures. Ileocolic resection for terminal ileal disease. Colectomy for colonic disease. Seton drainage for perianal fistulas. Proctectomy for severe perianal disease.
management.postop: Post-op prophylaxis is essential — recurrence is near 100% within 1 year endoscopically without treatment. Start Azathioprine or Anti-TNF within 2-4 weeks. Colonoscopy at 6-12 months to assess for endoscopic recurrence (Rutgeerts score). Smoking cessation mandatory. Nutritional support.

pathways.criteria: CDAI <220, no obstruction, no abscess, no fistula, no systemic toxicity
pathways.treatment: Budesonide for ileocecal disease. Prednisolone for colonic. Azathioprine for maintenance. Anti-TNF induction if no response in 2-4 weeks.

pathways.criteria: CDAI 220-450, active symptoms, inflammatory markers elevated, endoscopic activity
pathways.treatment: Anti-TNF (Infliximab/Adalimumab) ± Azathioprine. Step-down approach. Ustekinumab or Vedolizumab as second-line. Nutritional support (exclusive enteral nutrition in children).

pathways.criteria: abscess, fistula, obstruction, perforation, systemic illness, hypoalbuminemia
pathways.treatment: IV steroids. IV Anti-TNF (Infliximab). Percutaneous drainage of abscess. Surgical resection for stricture/fistula. Seton drainage for perianal fistula. Bowel resection with primary anastomosis. Stoma for severe disease.

operative_templates.indication: Strictured terminal ileum causing obstruction, or medically refractory ileocecal Crohn's disease
operative_templates.incision: Diagnostic laparoscopy with 4-5 ports; specimen extraction via Pfannenstiel or right lower quadrant incision
steps: Diagnostic laparoscopy — assess extent of disease
Identify the affected segment — terminal ileum and cecum
Mobilize right colon — white line of Toldt
Divide mesentery carefully — mesenteric bleeding risk (inflammatory tissue)
Identify and protect right ureter and duodenum
Divide terminal ileal mesentery with vessel ligation
Divide ileum 5-10cm proximal to the diseased segment
Divide colon distal to the cecum or at ascending colon
Extract specimen
Create ileocolic anastomosis — side-to-side functional end-to-end (stapled or handsewn)
Close mesenteric defect
Check haemostasis
Close wounds

operative_templates.indication: Complex transsphincteric, suprasphincteric, or Crohn's-related perianal fistula
operative_templates.incision: Probing of fistula tract — no incision, seton placed through the tract
steps: Position patient in lithotomy
Perform gentle digital rectal examination
Identify external opening(s) on perianal skin
Pass a probe (lacrimal probe or fistula probe) through external opening
Gently navigate the probe through the tract to identify internal opening
Confirm internal opening at dentate line — Goodsall's rule
Pass a vessel loop, silk suture, or silastic seton through the tract
Tie the seton loosely (non-cutting seton) for drainage
Record the anatomy (Parks classification)
Repeat EUA in 6-8 weeks or as needed

operative_templates.indication: Fibrotic small bowel stricture in Crohn's disease (preserve bowel length)
steps: Full small bowel run — identify all strictures, measure length
Select strictures for strictureplasty (skip segments where resection preferred)
For Heineke-Mikulicz (short strictures <10cm):
Longitudinal incision across the stricture onto normal bowel
Close transversely in 2 layers (Heineke-Mikulicz closure)
For Finney (longer strictures 10-25cm):
Fold the strictured segment on itself
Longitudinal enterotomy on both limbs
Side-to-side anastomosis
Ensure no tension or leaking
Close mesenteric defect if needed

dischargeCriteria: Stool output acceptable (not >6 liquid stools/day or <1 in obstructed)
Nutritional plan in place (oral supplements ± TPN)

patient_education.explanation: Crohn's disease is a chronic condition where the immune system attacks the lining of the digestive tract, causing inflammation, ulcers, and over time, narrowing (strictures) or tunnels (fistulas). It can affect any part from the mouth to the anus, but most commonly affects the last part of the small intestine and the beginning of the large intestine. There is no cure, but we can control it with medications and sometimes surgery. In Kenya, we always test for tuberculosis first, as it can mimic Crohn's very closely.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital immediately if you have: severe abdominal pain that does not go away, vomiting, fever with chills, abdominal swelling, inability to pass stool, drainage from a wound or the anus that increases, or severe bleeding from the back passage.
patient_education.activity: Stay active but avoid heavy lifting if you have a stoma or recent surgery. Gentle walking is encouraged. Avoid activities that increase abdominal pressure if you have fistulas. Rest during flares — listen to your body. Stay well hydrated, especially if you have diarrhea.
patient_education.diet: There is no one diet for Crohn's, but many find that avoiding spicy foods, raw vegetables, and dairy helps during flares. During flares: bland, low-fiber foods (rice, bananas, cooked vegetables, fish, chicken). Between flares: a balanced diet with all food groups. Avoid excessive fiber if you have strictures. Consider nutritional supplements if weight is low. In Kenya, traditional foods like ugali (moderate), rice, and cooked sukuma wiki are generally well tolerated.
patient_education.medication: This is a lifelong condition requiring lifelong medication. Do not stop your medications without consulting your doctor — stopping will cause a flare. If you are on Anti-TNF (Infliximab/Adalimumab): keep your infusion/injection schedule strictly. If on Azathioprine: monitor blood counts regularly. All patients need regular blood tests. Before starting Anti-TNF, ensure your TB test is negative.
patient_education.followUp: Regular follow-up with both a gastroenterologist and a colorectal surgeon is essential. Colonoscopy at 6-12 months after surgery to check for recurrence. Blood tests (CRP, CBC, albumin) every 3-6 months. If you have colonic Crohn's for 8-10 years, you need yearly colonoscopy cancer surveillance.

followup.before_discharge: Ensure patient tolerating oral intake. Check stoma/drain function. Review medications (biologic/immunomodulator plan). Confirm smoking cessation. Nutritional assessment. Arrange home care (stoma supplies if needed).

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check, seton/drain assessment. Review blood work (CRP, CBC). Start or restart biologic therapy. Smoking cessation reinforcement.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Clinical assessment of symptoms. Nutritional counseling. Optimize medical therapy. Coordinate with gastroenterology for long-term follow-up.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Assess for endoscopic recurrence — schedule colonoscopy at 6-12 months postoperatively. Adjust medications as needed. Assess perianal disease response to therapy.
after_discharge.3_months: Full clinical evaluation. Review calprotectin and CRP trends. Ensure regular surveillance plan. Long-term follow-up with gastroenterologist. Cancer surveillance initiation if >8 years of disease.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: severe abdominal pain, vomiting, fever, signs of obstruction (distension, constipation), new or worsening perianal pain/swelling, wound drainage or dehiscence, or any new fistula drainage.
